name: Upper Priest Lake
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding at scenic Upper Priest Lake with quartz and feldspar specimens. Flat terrain near water makes this an accessible northern Idaho collecting site.
description: Upper Priest Lake sits in the scenic panhandle of Idaho within Bonner County, offering public rockhounding on BLM land at moderate elevation. The flat terrain at 2442 feet elevation provides easy access for collectors of all skill levels seeking quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens typical of the glaciated northern Rocky Mountain region. This location combines peaceful lakeside setting with productive mineral collecting on designated public land. The area benefits from summer months when high mountain passes are accessible and weather is stable. Expect to find quality quartz crystals and feldspar fragments in the glacial deposits and talus slopes surrounding the lake. The combination of water access, flat walking terrain, and productive collecting make this a solid choice for family rockhounding trips in Idaho's remote panhandle.
history: Upper Priest Lake area was shaped by Pleistocene glaciation that carved the deep valleys and deposited glacial moraine containing granitic minerals from the Idaho Batholith. The quartz and feldspar specimens found here originate from Precambrian and Cretaceous granite bodies that underlie much of northern Idaho, exposed and fragmented by glacial action over the past 20000 years.
tips: Search the flatter ground near the lakeshore and in glacial deposits on south-facing slopes where winter snow melt exposes fresh material. Use a small hand sledge to carefully crack open quartz-rich rocks, and look for translucent or milky quartz clusters along old glacial terraces. Pack a small sieve to help identify feldspar fragments in the finer gravels, and bring water shoes or hiking boots rated for wet terrain since you'll be working near water and boggy areas.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently sloping terrain at 2442 feet elevation with mixed conifer forest and open areas near the lakeshore. Ground is moist in spring and early summer from snowmelt. Walking distance from parking is typically short (under 1 mile), with boggy patches and loose rocks along the shore. Footing is generally stable on exposed mineral-rich gravels but can be muddy in low areas.
best_season: June - September. The high elevation and northern latitude mean winter snow closes access and makes collecting impossible. Spring runoff creates boggy conditions; summer provides optimal weather and dry ground for productive rockhounding.
written_directions: From Newport, Idaho (nearest significant town, about 35 miles south), take US 2 north toward Priest River. Continue on ID 57 north through Priest River toward Upper Priest Lake Road. Follow Upper Priest Lake Road for approximately 25 miles into the lake area. Park near the north end of the lake where BLM public land is accessible. GPS coordinates (48.799145, -116.915548) mark productive collecting areas along the eastern shoreline.
road_conditions: Upper Priest Lake Road is a maintained rural road suitable for passenger vehicles in summer and fall. Road may have washboard sections and occasional rough patches. High clearance helpful but not required in dry conditions. Winter access is limited or impossible due to snow.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and small parking areas near the lake shore on BLM land. No developed lot; park off the main road near access points. Space is limited; arrive early during peak summer season.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Rockhounding is permitted on this BLM land for non-commercial mineral collecting. Pack out all trash and respect the pristine forest setting.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No power tools or heavy machinery
Pack out all trash
Respect forest closure signs
nearest_services: Newport, Idaho has gas, food, and supplies approximately 35 miles south. Priest River has basic services about 25 miles south. No services at Upper Priest Lake itself.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Unpaved road to parking area with informal pullouts. Boggy patches and loose rock near shoreline require sturdy footwear. Short walking distances from parking make this suitable for limited mobility visitors. No wheelchair-accessible facilities at site.

faq.question: What types of crystals can I find rockhounding at Upper Priest Lake Idaho?
faq.answer: Upper Priest Lake BLM land is known for quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Most commonly you will find milky quartz fragments and white feldspar pieces in glacial deposits and along the lakeshore. Clear quartz crystals can be found in deeper gravels with careful searching.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Upper Priest Lake in Bonner County?
faq.answer: No fee. Upper Priest Lake sits on public BLM land where recreational collecting is free. You only need to follow the 25 lb per day personal use limit and pack out your trash. No permit is required for casual rockhounding.

faq.question: When is the best time to go rockhounding near Priest Lake Idaho?
faq.answer: June through September is optimal. Winter snow closes access; spring runoff creates muddy terrain. Summer months provide dry ground, stable weather, and the most productive collecting conditions. September early fall offers cooler temperatures and fewer visitors.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and kids rockhounding at Upper Priest Lake?
faq.answer: Yes, Upper Priest Lake is very family-friendly. The flat terrain and short walking distances make it suitable for children as young as 6. The scenic lakeshore setting and abundant quartz specimens make it an engaging location for kids learning to rockhound. Bring water shoes for boggy areas.

faq.question: What tools do I need for rockhounding quartz at Upper Priest Lake?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand sledge or geology hammer, heavy-duty work gloves, and a collection bag. A small sieve helps identify feldspar in gravels. Sturdy hiking boots or water shoes are essential because the terrain near the lake is moist and rocky. A small magnifying glass helps identify mineral specimens.

faq.question: How do I get to Upper Priest Lake rockhounding from Newport Idaho?
faq.answer: From Newport take US 2 north toward Priest River, then ID 57 north through Priest River. Follow Upper Priest Lake Road north for about 25 miles. Park at informal pullouts on BLM land near the north end of the lake where public collecting is permitted. The GPS coordinates 48.799145, -116.915548 mark productive collecting areas.

meta_title: Upper Priest Lake Rockhounding - Idaho BLM
meta_description: Free rockhounding at Upper Priest Lake, Idaho. Collect quartz and feldspar on BLM land. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit. Family-friendly site.
